title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: key question developing simplified diagnostic criteria whether combinations optimized one sample generalize different population. PTSDdiag package supports derivation-validation framework : Optimal symptom combinations derived clinical sample (e.g., veterans high PTSD prevalence) combinations validated independent sample (e.g., general population lower prevalence) vignette demonstrates workflow using two simulated datasets included package: simulated_ptsd: clinical veteran sample (N = 5,000, ~94% PTSD prevalence) simulated_ptsd_genpop: general population sample (N = 1,200, ~21% PTSD prevalence) use optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() find 10 best six-symptom combinations veteran data, apply general population data using apply_symptom_combinations().

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
code: library("PTSDdiag")

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: derivation dataset represents clinical veteran sample high PTSD prevalence. load standardize column names. Let’s check PTSD prevalence sample:
code: # Load the veteran sample data("simulated_ptsd")  # Standardize column names veterans <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) veterans_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(veterans) veterans_dx <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(veterans_total) veterans_summary <- summarize_ptsd(veterans_dx) print(veterans_summary) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: validation dataset represents general population sample realistic, lower PTSD prevalence. Let’s check PTSD prevalence sample: prevalence difference two samples important: diagnostic metrics like PPV NPV strongly affected base rates. simplified criterion works well high-prevalence sample may perform differently individuals PTSD.
code: # Load the general population sample data("simulated_ptsd_genpop")  # Standardize column names genpop <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd_genpop) genpop_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(genpop) genpop_dx <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(genpop_total) genpop_summary <- summarize_ptsd(genpop_dx) print(genpop_summary) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1   23.63667 15.47307         252

code: # Define the PCL-5 cluster structure pcl5_clusters <- list(   B = 1:5,     # Intrusion   C = 6:7,     # Avoidance   D = 8:14,    # Negative alterations in cognitions and mood   E = 15:20    # Alterations in arousal and reactivity )  results_hier <- optimize_combinations_clusters(   veterans,   n_symptoms = 6,   n_required = 4,   n_top = 10,   score_by = "false_cases",   clusters = pcl5_clusters )  # View the 10 best symptom combinations results_hier$best_symptoms  # View diagnostic metrics on the derivation sample results_hier$summary

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: comparing derivation validation performance, several points important consider: Sensitivity may decrease validation sample simplified criteria overfit derivation sample’s specific symptom patterns. Specificity often changes substantially high-prevalence low-prevalence settings pool true negatives much larger general population. PPV (Positive Predictive Value) strongly influenced base rates. low-prevalence setting (~20%), even highly specific test false positives relative true positives. typically results lower PPV compared derivation sample. NPV (Negative Predictive Value) tends high low-prevalence settings negative results true negatives. Hierarchical vs. Non-Hierarchical: hierarchical approach may show higher specificity general population requiring cluster representation acts additional filter, reducing false positives. non-hierarchical approach may show higher sensitivity less restrictive.

title: External Validation of Simplified PTSD Criteria
text: apply_symptom_combinations() function enables straightforward derivation-validation workflow: derive optimal combinations one dataset test generalizability another. separating application step (creating diagnosis columns) summary step (summarize_ptsd_changes()), users full flexibility combine, compare, analyze results across multiple approaches datasets.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: PTSDdiag package provides tools analyzing optimizing PTSD diagnostic criteria using PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist DSM-5) data. Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) diagnosis traditionally requires complex evaluation multiple symptom criteria across different clusters. package aims simplify process maintaining diagnostic accuracy. vignette demonstrates use package : Import prepare PCL-5 data Calculate basic descriptive statistics reliability metrics Find optimal symptom combinations PTSD diagnosis Apply ICD-11 PTSD diagnostic criteria Compare diagnostic systems (DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, optimized combinations) unified table Export import optimized combinations Validate models using holdout cross-validation

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: can install released version CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("PTSDdiag") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("TobiasRSpiller/PTSDdiag")

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: PTSDdiag installed, can loaded usual way. Load additional packages:
code: library("PTSDdiag") library(psych)     # For reliability analysis

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: package includes simulated dataset mirrors hypothetical PCL-5-assessments. contains 5,000 simulated patient responses, rating 20 PTSD symptoms according DSM-5 criteria. Rating Scale symptom rated 5-point scale: 0 = 1 = little bit 2 = Moderately 3 = Quite bit 4 = Extremely Symptom Clusters PCL-5 organizes symptoms four distinct clusters according DSM-5 criteria: Symptoms 1-5: Criterion B (Intrusion) Symptoms 6-7: Criterion C (Avoidance) Symptoms 8-14: Criterion D (Negative alterations cognitions mood) Symptoms 15-20: Criterion E (Alterations arousal reactivity) Data Format Requirements Input data must : Numeric values 0-4 missing values 20 columns (one per symptom) Row-wise observations Let’s load included sample data:

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: first step standardize column names consistent analysis. standardization, columns might various names (S1–S20). standardization follow symptom_1–symptom_20 pattern expected package functions:
code: simulated_ptsd_renamed <- rename_ptsd_columns(simulated_ptsd) names(simulated_ptsd_renamed) #>  [1] "symptom_1"  "symptom_2"  "symptom_3"  "symptom_4"  "symptom_5"  #>  [6] "symptom_6"  "symptom_7"  "symptom_8"  "symptom_9"  "symptom_10" #> [11] "symptom_11" "symptom_12" "symptom_13" "symptom_14" "symptom_15" #> [16] "symptom_16" "symptom_17" "symptom_18" "symptom_19" "symptom_20"

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: process data three steps: calculate total scores, apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, summarize. summary provides mean total score, standard deviation, number DSM-5-diagnosed cases.
code: # Step 1: Calculate total scores (range 0-80) simulated_ptsd_total <- calculate_ptsd_total(simulated_ptsd_renamed)  # Step 2: Apply DSM-5 diagnostic criteria simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed <- create_ptsd_diagnosis_nonbinarized(simulated_ptsd_total)  # Step 3: Summary statistics summarize_ptsd(simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed) #>   mean_total sd_total n_diagnosed #> 1     57.772 12.36218        4710

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: Cronbach’s alpha assesses internal consistency PCL-5 items:
code: cronbach <- psych::alpha(subset(simulated_ptsd_total_diagnosed, select = -total)) round(cronbach$total, 3) #>  raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r    S/N   ase  mean    sd median_r #>      0.913     0.916   0.921     0.341 10.851 0.002 2.796 0.594    0.332

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: Current PTSD diagnosis requires evaluating 20 symptoms across four clusters complex rules. goal identify simplified diagnostic criteria : Reduce number symptoms evaluate Preserve polythetic nature diagnosis (minimum number selected symptoms must present) Maintain diagnostic accuracy compared original DSM-5 criteria identify optimal symptom combinations two structural approaches: hierarchical approach (requiring least one symptom DSM-5 cluster) non-hierarchical approach (ignoring cluster membership). Note computation time. examples use 4-symptom combinations (C(20, 4) = 4,845 evaluate), run seconds. typical real-world analysis uses 6-symptom combinations (C(20, 6) = 38,760), takes 1–2 minutes per call 5,000 observations. workflow output structure identical regardless n_symptoms.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: hierarchical approach least one symptom DSM-5 cluster must included combination. maintains structural logic diagnostic criteria. score_by argument controls “optimal” means: "newly_nondiagnosed" — minimize false negatives (missed diagnoses) "false_cases" — minimize total misclassifications function returns three elements. 1. best symptom combinations identified: 2. row-level comparison DSM-5-TR vs. new combination (first 5 rows shown): Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows) 3. Diagnostic accuracy metrics combination: Hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics summary includes total diagnosed/non-diagnosed counts, true/false positives negatives, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV.
code: best_combinations_hierarchical <- optimize_combinations_clusters(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   clusters   = list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20),   show_progress = FALSE ) best_combinations_hierarchical$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  7 11 17 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  4  6 11 17 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  1  6 11 17 knitr::kable(   head(best_combinations_hierarchical$diagnosis_comparison, 5),   caption = "Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows)" ) knitr::kable(   best_combinations_hierarchical$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: non-hierarchical approach selects symptoms purely accuracy, without requiring cluster representation. Best symptom combinations: Diagnosis comparison (first 5 rows): Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top non-hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows) Diagnostic accuracy metrics: Non-hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics
code: best_combinations_nonhierarchical <- optimize_combinations(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   show_progress = FALSE ) best_combinations_nonhierarchical$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  6  7 13 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  4  6  7 13 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  4  6  7 17 knitr::kable(   head(best_combinations_nonhierarchical$diagnosis_comparison, 5),   caption = "Diagnosis comparison: DSM-5-TR vs. top non-hierarchical combinations (first 5 rows)" ) knitr::kable(   best_combinations_nonhierarchical$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Non-hierarchical combinations: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: identifying optimal symptom combinations, natural next step benchmark established reference standards: full DSM-5-TR criteria ICD-11 PTSD definition. PTSDdiag package provides two functions purpose.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: ICD-11 defines PTSD using narrower parsimonious symptom set DSM-5-TR. Using PCL-5 items, ICD-11 requires three following clusters met (symptom present = score ≥ 2): create_icd11_diagnosis() applies criteria returns two-column data frame (PTSD_orig, PTSD_icd11) directly compatible existing metrics pipeline. output feeds directly standard metrics pipeline: ICD-11 vs. DSM-5-TR: diagnostic accuracy metrics
code: icd11_result <- create_icd11_diagnosis(simulated_ptsd_renamed) head(icd11_result) #>   PTSD_orig PTSD_icd11 #> 1      TRUE       TRUE #> 2      TRUE       TRUE #> 3      TRUE      FALSE #> 4      TRUE       TRUE #> 5      TRUE       TRUE #> 6      TRUE       TRUE cat(sprintf("DSM-5-TR prevalence: %.1f%%\n", mean(icd11_result$PTSD_orig)  * 100)) #> DSM-5-TR prevalence: 94.2% cat(sprintf("ICD-11 prevalence:   %.1f%%\n", mean(icd11_result$PTSD_icd11) * 100)) #> ICD-11 prevalence:   92.8% knitr::kable(   create_readable_summary(summarize_ptsd_changes(icd11_result)),   digits  = 4,   caption = "ICD-11 vs. DSM-5-TR: diagnostic accuracy metrics" )

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: compare_diagnostic_systems() combines number diagnostic criteria — DSM-5-TR (always reference), ICD-11 (computed internally icd11 = TRUE), pre-specified optimized combinations — single presentation-ready table. ICD-11 vs. DSM-5-TR : Comparison table: DSM-5-TR ICD-11 Adding optimized combinations: Pass comparison data frames apply_symptom_combinations() alongside raw data. can equally pass $diagnosis_comparison slot produced optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() — shares structure. reuse derivation results Section 4: Comparison table: DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, non-hierarchical hierarchical combinations Key points: "DSM-5-TR" "ICD-11" labels applied automatically — need specify labels. Use labels argument rename optimized combination rows (default, column names symptom_1_6_8_15 used). Set icd11 = FALSE omit ICD-11 row needed. function validates PTSD_orig columns match reference computed data, ensuring consistency.
code: knitr::kable(   compare_diagnostic_systems(simulated_ptsd_renamed, icd11 = TRUE),   digits  = 4,   caption = "Comparison table: DSM-5-TR and ICD-11" ) tbl_full <- compare_diagnostic_systems(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   best_combinations_nonhierarchical$diagnosis_comparison,   best_combinations_hierarchical$diagnosis_comparison,   icd11 = TRUE )  knitr::kable(   tbl_full,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Comparison table: DSM-5-TR, ICD-11, non-hierarchical and hierarchical combinations" )

code: # Save to a temporary file for this demonstration # In practice, specify a permanent path: file = "hierarchical_combos.json" tmp_hier    <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") tmp_nonhier <- tempfile(fileext = ".json")  write_combinations(   best_combinations_hierarchical$best_symptoms,   file        = tmp_hier,   n_required  = 3,   clusters    = list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20),   score_by    = "newly_nondiagnosed",   description = "Top 3 hierarchical combinations from simulated data" )  write_combinations(   best_combinations_nonhierarchical$best_symptoms,   file       = tmp_nonhier,   n_required = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   description = "Top 3 non-hierarchical combinations from simulated data" )  cat("Files written successfully.\n") #> Files written successfully.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: identifying optimal symptom combinations, crucial validate performance independent data. PTSDdiag package provides two validation approaches. Note computation time. examples use n_symptoms = 4 keep build time short. Real-world analyses typically use n_symptoms = 6.

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: Holdout validation splits data training (70%) test (30%) sets. Optimal combinations derived training set evaluated held-test set. Best combinations identified training data: Test-set performance: Holdout validation: non-hierarchical combinations (test set) Holdout validation: hierarchical combinations (test set)
code:
validation_results <- holdout_validation(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   train_ratio = 0.7,   score_by    = "newly_nondiagnosed",   seed        = 123,   n_symptoms  = 4,   n_required  = 3,   n_top       = 3 ) cat("Non-hierarchical:
") #> Non-hierarchical: print(validation_results$without_clusters$best_combinations) #> [[1]] #> [1]  4  6  7 13 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  1  6  7 13 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  4  6  7 17 cat("
Hierarchical:
") #>  #> Hierarchical: print(validation_results$with_clusters$best_combinations) #> [[1]] #> [1]  4  6 11 17 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  1  6 11 17 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  5  6 11 17 knitr::kable(   validation_results$without_clusters$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Holdout validation: non-hierarchical combinations (test set)" ) knitr::kable(   validation_results$with_clusters$summary,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Holdout validation: hierarchical combinations (test set)" )

title: An introduction to PTSDdiag
text: k-fold cross-validation provides robust estimate generalization repeating train/test split k times. Results fold — non-hierarchical: Cross-validation: non-hierarchical results fold Results fold — hierarchical: Cross-validation: hierarchical results fold Stable combinations across folds — combination appeared multiple folds, average performance reported: Stable non-hierarchical combinations across folds Stable hierarchical combinations across folds Interpreting cross-validation results: Consistency — whether combinations selected across folds Stability — diagnostic accuracy varies across folds Generalization — average metrics provide robust real-world estimates Key metrics: Splits_Appeared (many folds selected combination), sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV.
code: cv_results <- cross_validation(   simulated_ptsd_renamed,   k          = 3,   score_by   = "newly_nondiagnosed",   seed       = 123,   n_symptoms = 4,   n_required = 3,   n_top      = 3 ) knitr::kable(   cv_results$without_clusters$summary_by_fold,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Cross-validation: non-hierarchical results by fold" ) knitr::kable(   cv_results$with_clusters$summary_by_fold,   digits  = 4,   caption = "Cross-validation: hierarchical results by fold" ) if (!is.null(cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   knitr::kable(     cv_results$without_clusters$combinations_summary,     digits  = 4,     caption = "Stable non-hierarchical combinations across folds"   ) } else {   cat("No combination appeared in more than one fold (non-hierarchical).\n") } if (!is.null(cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary)) {   knitr::kable(     cv_results$with_clusters$combinations_summary,     digits  = 4,     caption = "Stable hierarchical combinations across folds"   ) } else {   cat("No combination appeared in more than one fold (hierarchical).\n") }

", holdout_sens)) #> Holdout sensitivity (top non-hierarchical combo): 0.9626 cat(sprintf("CV mean sensitivity across folds:                 %.4f
", mean(cv_fold_sens, na.rm = TRUE))) #> Warning in mean.default(cv_fold_sens, na.rm = TRUE): argument is not numeric or #> logical: returning NA #> CV mean sensitivity across folds:                 NA cat(sprintf("CV sensitivity range:                             %.4f – %.4f

Apply combinations (non-hierarchical) comparison <- apply_symptom_combinations(ptsd_data, my_combinations,                 n_required = 4)  # Compute metrics metrics <- summarize_ptsd_changes(comparison) create_readable_summary(metrics) #>                 Scenario Total Diagnosed Total Non-Diagnosed True Positive #> 1              PTSD_orig        11 (55%)             9 (45%)            11 #> 2 symptom_1_6_8_10_15_19         9 (45%)            11 (55%)             8 #> 3 symptom_2_7_9_11_16_20         9 (45%)            11 (55%)             7 #>   True Negative Newly Diagnosed Newly Non-Diagnosed True Cases False Cases #> 1             9               0                   0         20           0 #> 2             8               1                   3         16           4 #> 3             7               2                   4         14           6 #>   Sensitivity Specificity    PPV    NPV #> 1      1.0000      1.0000 1.0000 1.0000 #> 2      0.7273      0.8889 0.8889 0.7273 #> 3      0.6364      0.7778 0.7778 0.6364  # title: Apply ICD-11 PTSD diagnostic criteria to PCL-5 data — create_icd11_diagnosis
text: ICD-11 PTSD requires THREE following clusters met (symptom present = score \(\ge\) 2 original 0–4 scale): Re-experiencing (present): \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5     items 1, 2, 3 (intrusive memories, nightmares, flashbacks) Avoidance: \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5 items 6, 7 Sense current threat: \(\ge\) 1 PCL-5 items 16, 17     (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle) minimum 3 symptoms total across ICD-11 items (1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 16, 17) must present. automatically satisfied three cluster requirements met enforced explicitly clarity. DSM-5-TR diagnosis (PTSD_orig) computed using binarization logic rest package (create_ptsd_diagnosis_binarized).

Cluster   elements must overlap. parameter required (default). PCL-5:   list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20) DT Logical. TRUE, return summary interactive datatable widget. FALSE (default), return plain data.frame. DT package must installed DT = TRUE. show_progress Logical. TRUE (default), display progress bar evaluating combinations. Set FALSE batch non-interactive use.

title: Find optimal symptom combinations for diagnosis (hierarchical/cluster-based) — optimize_combinations_clusters
text: function: Generates valid combinations ensuring representation clusters Requires n_required symptoms present (>=2 original 0-4   scale) diagnosis Validates present symptoms include least one cluster Identifies n_top combinations best match original   DSM-5 diagnosis clusters parameter must named list specifying cluster structure. PCL-5, standard clusters : Cluster B (Intrusion): Items 1-5 Cluster C (Avoidance): Items 6-7 Cluster D (Negative alterations cognitions mood): Items 8-14 Cluster E (Alterations arousal reactivity): Items 15-20 Optimization can based either: Minimizing false cases (false positives false negatives) Minimizing false negatives (newly non-diagnosed cases)

title: Find optimal symptom combinations for diagnosis (hierarchical/cluster-based) — optimize_combinations_clusters
code: # Create example data ptsd_data <- data.frame(matrix(sample(0:4, 200, replace=TRUE), ncol=20)) names(ptsd_data) <- paste0("symptom_", 1:20)  # \donttest{ # Find best hierarchical combinations with PCL-5 clusters pcl5_clusters <- list(B = 1:5, C = 6:7, D = 8:14, E = 15:20) results <- optimize_combinations_clusters(ptsd_data, n_symptoms = 6,              n_required = 4, score_by = "false_cases", clusters = pcl5_clusters) #> ℹ Generated 13685 valid cluster-constrained combinations #> ℹ Evaluated 13685 combinations. Best: 1, 4, 6, 9, 15, 20  # Get symptom numbers results$best_symptoms #> [[1]] #> [1]  1  4  6  9 15 20 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1]  2  4  6  9 15 20 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1]  1  4  6  9 17 18 #>   # View summary statistics results$summary #>                Scenario combination_id rank Total Diagnosed Total Non-Diagnosed #> 1             PTSD_orig           <NA>   NA       10 (100%)              0 (0%) #> 2 symptom_1_4_6_9_15_20  1_4_6_9_15_20    1         7 (70%)             3 (30%) #> 3 symptom_2_4_6_9_15_20  2_4_6_9_15_20    2         7 (70%)             3 (30%) #> 4 symptom_1_4_6_9_17_18  1_4_6_9_17_18    3         7 (70%)             3 (30%) #>   True Positive True Negative Newly Diagnosed Newly Non-Diagnosed True Cases #> 1            10             0               0                   0         10 #> 2             7             0               0                   3          7 #> 3             7             0               0                   3          7 #> 4             7             0               0                   3          7 #>   False Cases Sensitivity Specificity PPV NPV #> 1           0         1.0          NA   1  NA #> 2           3         0.7          NA   1   0 #> 3           3         0.7          NA   1   0 #> 4           3         0.7          NA   1   0 # }

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.1
text: optimize_combinations(), optimize_combinations_clusters(), holdout_validation(), cross_validation() now return plain data frames default. Set DT = TRUE interactive DT widgets (previous default behaviour). Combination identity (combination_id rank columns) now tracked full pipeline: optimization summaries, write_combinations() / read_combinations() JSON files, cross-validation combination summaries. Progress reporting via cli package: fold-level progress bars cross_validation() informational messages holdout_validation() optimization functions. cross_validation() supports parallel fold processing via future.apply available. Set future::plan(future::multisession) calling.

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.1
text: analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required() analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required_clusters() soft-deprecated favour optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters().

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.1
text: Updated vignettes use generalized API (optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters()) instead legacy wrappers.

title: PTSDdiag 0.2.0
text: New generalized optimization functions optimize_combinations() optimize_combinations_clusters() allow configurable number symptoms per combination (n_symptoms), required threshold (n_required), number top results (n_top), custom cluster definitions (clusters). New apply_symptom_combinations() function external validation: apply pre-derived combinations new dataset compare DSM-5 baseline. New write_combinations() read_combinations() functions exporting importing optimized symptom combinations human-readable JSON files, enabling collaboration across research groups without sharing raw data. Original functions analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required() analyze_best_six_symptoms_four_required_clusters() preserved backward-compatible wrappers PCL-5 defaults. holdout_validation() cross_validation() now accept n_symptoms, n_required, n_top parameters. Internal refactoring: shared validation diagnosis logic consolidated reusable internal helpers.

title: PTSDdiag 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-02-13 Initial CRAN submission. Core analysis functions identifying optimal 6-symptom PTSD diagnostic combinations using PCL-5 data. Hierarchical (cluster-based) non-hierarchical analysis approaches. DSM-5 diagnostic criteria implementation (binarized non-binarized). Diagnostic accuracy metrics: sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV. Holdout k-fold cross-validation methods model evaluation. Simulated PCL-5 dataset 5,000 observations demonstration.
